info.structure: Part 33: Chapter 2, Section 21. The Meditation on the All-Inclusive Universal Sāman (Sarva-Sāmopāsanā). This section transitions from individual named melodies to an absolute synthesis, where the entire created universe, all structural elements, and all previous meditations are integrated into a single, comprehensive vision of the Sāman.
info.format: Devanagari, IAST, English translation, and philosophical commentaries.

sections.title: The Meditation on the All-Inclusive Universal Sāman

verses.devanagari: त्रयी विद्या हिंकारस्त्रयो लोकाः स प्रस्तावोऽग्निर्वायुरादित्याः स उद्गीथो नक्षत्राणि वयांसि मरीचयः स प्रतिहारः सर्पा गन्धर्वाः पितरस्तन्निधनमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतम् ॥ १ ॥
verses.iast: trayī vidyā hiṃkārastrayo lokāḥ sa prastāvo'gnirvāyu rādityāḥ sa udgītho nakṣatrāṇi vayāṃsi marīcayaḥ sa pratihāraḥ sarpā gandharvāḥ pitarastannidhanametatsāma sarvasminprotam || 1 ||
verses.english_translation: The triple Vedic knowledge (Ṛg, Yajus, Sāma) is the syllable 'Hiṃ'; the three worlds (Earth, Atmosphere, Heaven) are the introductory praise (Prastāva); Fire, Wind, and Sun are the loud chant (Udgītha); the Stars, Birds, and Light-rays are the response (Pratihāra); and the Serpents, Celestial Musicians, and Ancestors are the finale (Nidhana). This is the all-inclusive Sāman as woven into everything.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Adi Śaṅkara notes that after detailing ten specific, localized variations of the Sāman, the Upanishad now opens the door to the ultimate *Sarva-Sāma*—the meditation on the universe as a singular, complete chant. The text creates groups of three to bind all vertical and horizontal layers of reality into the five chant divisions. The foundational grid of revelation, the *Trayī Vidyā* (the three Vedas), acts as *Hiṃkāra*. The spatial fields where life unfolds, the *Trayo Lokāḥ* (the three worlds), are the *Prastāva*. The primary energetic forces that light and move those worlds—Fire, Wind, and Sun—are crowned as the majestic *Udgītha*. The secondary cosmic networks—Stars, Birds in the air, and Light-rays—serve as the balancing *Pratihāra*. Finally, the subtler, hidden layers of life—Serpents (*sarpāḥ*), Gandharvas, and the Ancestors (*pitaraḥ*)—act as the *Nidhana*. Shankara explains that this total vision removes all sense of division; the seeker no longer sees separate pieces of matter, but a single, harmonized web where *Sarvam* (everything) is the Sāman itself.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that this all-inclusive grouping is an expression of the *Viśvarūpa* or cosmic body of the Supreme Lord. The universe is not an accidental collection of elements, but a beautifully orchestrated divine hierarchy. By bringing scriptural knowledge, physical worlds, celestial controllers, and ancestral spirits into one single musical matrix, the text guides the soul to see that everything is owned, filled, and harmonized by the indwelling Lord.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhvācārya points out that this universal integration is administered by the primary life-force, Mukhyaprāṇa, working directly under the absolute sovereignty of Lord Viṣṇu. The phrase *sarvasmin protam* (woven into everything) serves as a reminder of the universe's total dependency. No layer of reality—from the highest Veda to the lowest creeping serpent—can move or exist outside the rigid cosmic layout established by the independent will of God.

verses.devanagari: स य एवमेतत्साम सर्वस्मिन्प्रोतं वेद सर्वं ह भवति ॥ २ ॥
verses.iast: sa ya evametatsāma sarvasminprotaṃ veda sarvaṃ ha bhavati || 2 ||
verses.english_translation: He who knows this all-inclusive Sāman as woven into everything becomes everything (Sarvaṃ ha bhavati).

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara delivers a definitive non-dual commentary on *sarvaṃ ha bhavati* (he becomes everything). This is the apex reward of the meditation. When a practitioner completely drops their narrow identity as a single body and instead views every sound, world, creature, and light-ray as the unfolding of one vast Sāman chant, their ego-boundaries collapse. To realize that the cosmic harmony is identical to your inner Self is to *become* everything. This is not a physical shape-shifting, but an absolute expansion of consciousness where nothing remains separate from you, marking the transition from ritual tracking to liberation (*Mukti*).
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school explains that 'becoming everything' signifies the soul attaining the state of *Sādharmya*—sharing fully in the cosmic perspective, unconditioned joy, and infinite awareness of the Supreme Lord. The devotee is completely freed from the tiny prison of selfishness, experiencing the entire universe as an extension of God's glory and a playground for his own liberated consciousness.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva clarifies that a finite individual soul (*jīva*) can never literally become the infinite God. In this context, *sarvaṃ bhavati* means that the liberated soul gains unhindered access to all realms of creation and enjoys perfect harmony with the entire universe through the grace of Viṣṇu. Their will is perfectly aligned with the divine layout, so they face no boundaries, obstructions, or sorrow anywhere in existence.

verses.devanagari: तदेष श्लोको यदेषां पञ्चधा पञ्चधा त्रैण्यानि न तेभ्यस्त्यात्परमन्यदस्ति ॥ ३ ॥
verses.iast: tadeṣa śloko yadeṣāṃ pañcadhā pañcadhā traiṇyāni na tebhyastyātparamanyadasti || 3 ||
verses.english_translation: On this point there is this verse: 'There are things that are grouped by fives in three ways, and nothing exists that is higher or different from them.'

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara unpacks this dense, cryptic summary verse. The phrase *pañcadhā pañcadhā traiṇyāni* refers to the three distinct ways the fivefold Sāman categories have been mapped across reality: first to the cosmic worlds (Earth, Atmosphere, etc.), second to the physiological elements (Breath, Speech, Senses), and third to the elemental triads (Fire, Wind, Sun). By saying *na tebhyaḥ param asti* (nothing exists higher than them), the text confirms that this system encompasses all manifest reality. For the meditator, this means the entire universe has been gathered, organized, and dissolved into the Sāman framework, leaving no room for a separate, distracting world outside of Brahman.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: The school notes that this verse establishes the flawless completeness of the Lord's design. The threefold groupings of five represent the structural building blocks of the cosmic matrix (*Prapañca*). By recognizing that nothing exists outside this divine architecture, the devotee's mind settles into a state of total, restful surrender, seeing every experience as an interaction within the organized body of God.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: Madhva explains that this closing verse asserts the total sovereignty of the divine layout. The triads and fivefold classifications prove that creation is meticulously calculated and bound by numbers and laws. Nothing can slip through the cracks or operate independently of the primary rule of Viṣṇu, ensuring that the seeker can completely trust the cosmic government managed by the Supreme Person.

verses.devanagari: यस्तं वेद स सर्वविद्भवति न मां प्रति दिशो बलिं हरन्तीति सर्वमस्मीत्युपासीत तद्व्रतम् ॥ ४ ॥
verses.iast: yastaṃ veda sa sarvavidbhavati na māṃ prati diśo baliṃ harantīti sarvamasmītyupāseeta tadvratam || 4 ||
verses.english_translation: He who knows this becomes the knower of everything (Sarvavit). All the cardinal directions bring tribute and offerings to him. His sacred vow should be to meditate with the awareness: 'I am everything' (Sarvam asmīti). That is the vow.

commentaries.advaita_vedanta: Shankara tracks the final, supreme behavior vow (*Vrata*) of this section: *sarvam asmīti upāsīta* (one must meditate knowing 'I am all'). This is no longer a localized rule about not spitting or complaining; it is an absolute demand on identity. The sage must walk through the world with the unshakeable awareness that whatever they see, hear, or touch is an extension of their own universal being. The text notes that *diśo baliṃ haranti*—all directions bring tribute to him. Because he no longer fights the world as an outsider, the elements naturally serve, support, and honor him. He is *Sarvavit*, the knower of everything, because by knowing the underlying Sāman, he knows the heart of all things.
commentaries.vishishtadvaita: Raṅgarāmānuja writes that when a devotee reaches this state of complete union, the whole world honors them because they see the Lord shining purely through them. The phrase 'I am everything' is understood from the perspective of the indwelling soul: 'Because the Lord is everything, and the Lord is my innermost Self, I am structurally unified with all creation.' This vow eliminates all pride, fear, and sense of lack, bathing the devotee in endless divine prosperity.
commentaries.dvaita_vedanta: According to Madva, the title *Sarvavit* (all-knowing) is granted to the devotee by the grace of Lord Viṣṇu, who reveals the inner mechanics of creation to his closest servants. The directions bringing tribute means that the planetary and directional deities (*Dikpālas*) cooperate with and protect the enlightened soul. The vow 'I am all' is practiced as an act of total surrender: 'I am completely filled, possessed, and sustained by the All-Pervading Lord, and my independent ego is nothing.' This keeps the seeker in a state of supreme humility and power, concluding the meditation chapter on a note of absolute victory.
